Snorkelers cross water thick with golden jellyfish (Mastigias papua etpisoni), an endemic subspecies that lost most of its sting in isolation and pulses across the lake each day to keep its symbiotic algae in sunlight. Moon jellyfish drift through the same surface layer. Scuba is banned: exhaled bubbles lodge under the bells and harm the animals, and a hydrogen sulfide layer below 15 m is dangerous to breathe near. Golden jellyfish numbers swing with climate, from tens of millions in good years to near total collapse and back.
getThere: Koror (ROR / Roman Tmetuchl International) is Palau's only international airport, with flights from Manila, Taipei, Seoul, Guam, and Tokyo. The lake sits on Eil Malk island in the southern Rock Islands, roughly a 45 minute boat ride south of Koror followed by a short uphill climb over the ridge to the water. Most Koror day boats include it on a Rock Islands snorkel itinerary.

siteSpecificGear.name: Snorkel and mask (no scuba)
siteSpecificGear.reason: Scuba is prohibited in the lake; the visit is a surface snorkel, so mask, snorkel and fins are all you take in.

siteSpecificGear.name: Rash guard
siteSpecificGear.reason: A thin layer guards skin against incidental contact with the mild jellyfish and against sun on the long surface swim.

notes: Permits: requires the Rock Islands / Koror State permit plus a separate Jellyfish Lake permit, usually bundled by operators with the Palau Pristine Paradise Environmental Fee paid on arrival. Snorkel only, no scuba. Rinse off sunscreen and oils before entering to protect the lake. Golden jellyfish densities have crashed and recovered repeatedly with El Niño warming, so the spectacle varies widely by year; confirm current status before booking.

name: Numidia Wreck
description: A 137 m Anchor Line steamer that ran straight onto the north point of Big Brother in July 1901 and slid down the reef wall, where she now lies almost vertical — bow at 8 m, stern past 80 m. Two railway axles and their iron wheels still sit on the deck near 25 m, cargo that never reached Calcutta. Soft corals and gorgonians sheath the hull, clouds of glassfish fill the holds, and anthias hang over the rails while grey reef sharks patrol the blue off the stern.
getThere: Liveaboard-only, departing Hurghada (HRG) or Marsa Alam (RMF), both with direct European flights. Southern itineraries that take in Brothers, Daedalus and Elphinstone run roughly 5 to 7 nights and reach the El Ikhwa (Brother) Islands offshore in the central Red Sea.
notes: The wreck drops down a vertical wall: bow around 8 m, the railway wheels near 25 m, midship by 40 to 50 m and the stern beyond 80 m, so the lower hull is technical only. Brother Islands are liveaboard-only and the marine park is generally reachable only in the calmer months.

name: Bimini Great Hammerheads
description: From December to March, great hammerheads up to 4 m long patrol a shallow white sand flat on the lee side of South Bimini, the most reliable encounter with this species anywhere in the Atlantic. Divers kneel on the bottom at around 6 m while a baited handler draws the sharks in to glide past at arm's length, scalloped heads sweeping the sand. Nurse sharks and wintering bull sharks crowd the same flat, and the Gulf Stream runs along the deep edge just offshore. Visibility stays high over the pale sand, making this one of the great wide angle shark photography dives.
getThere: South Bimini Airport (BIM) is a 25 minute flight from Fort Lauderdale, or reach Bimini by fast ferry from Miami. The hammerhead flat is a short boat ride on the lee side of South Bimini, about 5 minutes from the island resorts and dive centers.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Divers kneel still on the sand for the whole dive; winter water near 23C chills you without the extra neoprene.

siteSpecificGear.name: Wide angle camera
siteSpecificGear.reason: Four metre hammerheads pass within arm's reach over bright sand, the classic close focus wide angle shot.

notes: Baited, guided shark dive run by Bimini operators; the great hammerhead season runs roughly November to April and peaks January to March. Expect a long, shallow dive kneeling on sand while sharks are drawn in with bait. Nurse and bull sharks share the site, and a Gulf Stream current runs along the nearby drop. This is a pelagic encounter dive, not a reef or coral site.

name: Manta Ray Night Dive (Garden Eel Cove)
description: By day this sandy shelf off Keahole Point is Garden Eel Cove, named for the colonies of Hawaiian garden eels that sway from the bottom near 12 m. After dark it becomes Manta Heaven: crews set powerful lights on the sand at about 11 m, the glow concentrates plankton into a bright column, and resident reef mantas with wingspans near 4 m sweep in to barrel roll through the swarm, mouths agape, sometimes passing inches above kneeling divers. Kona's catalog holds more than 200 identified individuals, so the show runs most nights of the year. A resident undulated moray nicknamed Frank often turns up to pick off fish drawn to the lights. Keahole Point shelters the cove from the summer south swell.
getThere: Fly into Ellison Onizuka Kona International Airport (KOA) at Keahole, minutes from the site. Manta night dive boats leave Honokohau Harbor just north of Kailua Kona and reach Garden Eel Cove in 20 to 30 minutes. The Keauhou Bay site, known as Manta Village, is the alternate to the south when swell favors it. Shore entry over lava rock is possible but most divers go by boat.

siteSpecificGear.name: Backup dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Every diver carries a primary and a backup light for the night descent, even though crews set the big plankton lights on the sand.

siteSpecificGear.name: 5mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: You kneel motionless on the sand for 30 to 45 minutes while the mantas feed, so divers chill faster than the warm water suggests, especially on winter nights.

notes: This is a night dive: bring a primary and backup light, though operators supply the bottom lights that draw the plankton. The dive is shallow and stationary, so divers get cold quickly. Touching mantas is prohibited and strips their protective mucus; stay low, keep your arms in, and let them pass over you. Garden Eel Cove off the airport is the more sheltered choice in summer, while Keauhou Bay can be exposed to south swell.

name: Garden of Eden
description: A wall and coral plateau off the western tip of Qesmah, one of the nine Daymaniyat islands scattered 70 km off Muscat. Boulders and blocks spangled with orange, purple and yellow soft corals drop from a 12 m shelf to sand near 23 m. Green and hawksbill turtles graze the plateau, which sits inside a reserve set aside in 1996 to protect their nesting beaches, and whale sharks cruise through on the late summer plankton bloom.
getThere: Fly into Muscat International (MCT), Oman's main hub with direct links across Europe, Asia and the Gulf. Dive boats run out to the islands in about 60 to 90 minutes from marinas just north of the city, such as Al Mouj and Seeb.
notes: The Daymaniyat Islands are a protected nature reserve. Landing is closed during turtle and seabird nesting season (roughly May to October), and all diving must be booked through licensed Muscat operators. Carry an SMB for boat traffic inside the reserve.

name: San Miguel Island
description: San Miguel is the westernmost and most exposed of the Channel Islands, where the cold California Current slams into the coast and feeds dense giant kelp. Point Bennett on the western tip holds the largest pinniped haul out on the planet, with California and Steller sea lions, northern fur seals, harbor seals and elephant seals piled by the thousand. Drop into the kelp off Tyler Bight and curious young sea lions corkscrew through the golden canopy at arm's length. The classic dive is Wyckoff Ledge, a rock wall falling from 12 m to a sand floor near 27 m and crusted with strawberry anemones, nudibranchs, wolf eels and lingcod. Water hovers around 11 to 13 C and the seas are unforgiving, so the island only opens on calm days.

conditionsByMonth.suitRecommendation: Drysuit, or 7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves
Drysuit, or 7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves
Drysuit, or 7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves
7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves, or drysuit
7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves, or drysuit
Drysuit, or 7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves

getThere: Fly into Santa Barbara (SBA) or Los Angeles (LAX). Dive boats run from Santa Barbara Harbor and Ventura Harbor. San Miguel is the most distant of the Channel Islands, a crossing of roughly 3 to 5 hours, so it is almost always reached on multi day liveaboard charters rather than day trips. There are no facilities on the island.
notes: Part of Channel Islands National Park and National Marine Sanctuary; the island is owned by the US Navy and managed by the National Park Service. Going ashore needs a permit and a ranger escort, and Point Bennett is closed to protect breeding wildlife, so the diving is done from the boat offshore. Conditions are exposed with strong swell and current and the island is frequently weathered out, calmest from late summer into fall. Cold water calls for a 7mm wetsuit with hood and gloves or a drysuit. Never approach hauled out pinnipeds.

name: Peleliu Corner
description: Peleliu marks the southern tip of Palau's barrier reef, where Pacific Ocean and Philippine Sea currents collide over a submarine ridge between Peleliu and Angaur, generating some of the strongest flow in Palau. The dive traces the sheer Peleliu Wall, dropping past giant sea fans from a 10 m plateau near the World War II landing beach monument to a 30 m corner where Peleliu Cut and Peleliu Express merge. Divers set a reef hook at the lip and hold against the rip while grey reef sharks cruise the blue in groups of 15 or more, joined by whitetips, bumphead parrotfish, Napoleon wrasse, and schooling barracuda. Slack tide windows are short and the current can pull divers deep into the corner, so trips are timed to the tide tables. Sailfin snapper mass to spawn here from March through May.
getThere: Fly into Roman Tmetuchl International Airport (ROR) near Koror via Manila, Taipei, Tokyo, or Guam. Peleliu lies about 56 km south of Koror at the bottom of the barrier reef; most divers reach it on a long speedboat run from Koror or by liveaboard working the southern reefs, while a small island airfield and ferry serve those staying on Peleliu itself.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents are among the strongest in Palau and reverse at the tide change. Hooking into rubble at the 10 to 15 m lip is the only way to hold position and watch the shark line without finning hard.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits run out over open ocean off the corner, so operators require an SMB deployed on ascent for boat pickup.

notes: Permit: Koror State Rock Islands pass plus the Palau Pristine Paradise Environmental Fee, paid on arrival. Advanced certification with drift and reef hook experience expected. Currents are tide driven and can run hard with strong downcurrents near the corner, so dives are scheduled around slack water; follow the tide and exit briefing closely. Often combined with WWII wreck dives and topside battlefield visits.

name: Rainbow Warrior
description: Greenpeace's flagship, bombed by French secret service agents in Auckland Harbour in 1985 in an attack that killed photographer Fernando Pereira, now rests off the Cavalli Islands near Matauri Bay. The salvaged hull was scuttled here in December 1987 as an artificial reef, the masts removed and displayed onshore. The 40 m wreck lies upright in 27 m of water with the deck around 16 m, its steel skin smothered in a dense carpet of varicoloured jewel anemones that give the dive its nickname, Jewel of the North. Schools of snapper, blue maomao and demoiselles hang over the wreck while kingfish patrol the blue and john dory hover near the superstructure. Cooler subtropical water and depth make this an advanced dive, though current is usually mild.
getThere: Fly into Auckland or Kerikeri (Bay of Islands), then drive to Matauri Bay or Tutukaka in Northland. Dive charters run to the wreck from Matauri Bay (about 10 minutes by boat to the Cavalli Islands) and on day trips from Paihia and Tutukaka.
notes: The wreck lies within a marine area managed as a fish sanctuary; no fishing or souvenir collecting. Depth to 27 m and cool water make this an advanced dive; penetration of the hull requires wreck training and a guide. Surface conditions in exposed Northland water can build quickly, so trips depend on weather windows.

name: Saco do Cardeiro
description: A sheltered cove on the calm inner sea of Arraial do Cabo, the mar de dentro, where a rocky bottom slopes gently from about 6 to 11 meters under unusually clear water. Cold, plankton rich pulses from the South Atlantic upwelling feed dense schools of bigeye and other fish that drift over reef carpeted in zoanthids, gorgonians and sponges. Divers come for the resident longsnout seahorses and for a bright orange and pink soft coral, not native to Brazil, that has colonised the rocks and become a favorite subject for underwater photographers. Barracuda, green turtles, small morays and the occasional dolphin pass through. Gentle depth and protected water make this Arraial's main training and discover scuba site.
getThere: Fly into Cabo Frio Airport (CFB), about 25 minutes by road, or Rio de Janeiro Galeao (GIG), roughly 2.5 hours by car or bus. Dive boats leave the Praia dos Anjos pier in Arraial do Cabo; the cove is about a 15 minute ride into the sheltered inner sea.
notes: Shallow, sheltered cove that operators use for discover scuba and certification dives, so expect training groups. Departures are by boat from the Praia dos Anjos pier, and the area lies within the Arraial do Cabo Marine Extractive Reserve. Water can cool sharply when upwelling drives cold water inshore, even in Brazilian summer, so carry enough exposure protection.

name: Boiler Wreck (Abu Madafi)
description: Abu Madafi is an offshore reef about 30 minutes by boat north of Jeddah, and its signature dive is the Boiler Wreck, a steamer that has lain here so long that little remains beyond a section of the stern and the two heavy iron boilers it is named for, now thickly crusted in hard and soft coral. The wreckage rests in 4 to 20 meters on a reef pierced by a network of caves and narrow passages where shafts of sunlight cut through openings overhead, and a coral wall drops away alongside into deeper water. Moderate current pulls in whitetip and blacktip reef sharks, great barracuda, dogtooth tuna and jacks, with reef mantas passing through in the cooler months. It is the most dived site in Jeddah and an approachable introduction to Saudi Red Sea diving.
getThere: Fly into King Abdulaziz International Airport (JED) in Jeddah. Dive operators run boats from the marinas at Obhur (Sharm Obhur) on the northern edge of the city, and it is roughly a 30 minute ride out to the Abu Madafi reef.
notes: Boat dive on an open reef with moderate current, so it suits divers comfortable with mild drift. Several wrecks lie on Abu Madafi besides the Boiler Wreck, including the Old Coaster and the Pipe Wreck, and the reef is riddled with caves and passages that reward good buoyancy and a torch. Coordinates are approximate for an offshore reef reached by boat from Obhur. Diving in Saudi Arabia is arranged through licensed local operators.

name: Crystal Rock
description: A current swept pinnacle in the far north of Komodo National Park, 300 m from Castle Rock, rising from open water to break the surface at low tide. A submerged spur falls away to a saddle near 20 m, its walls stacked with giant sea fans, barrel sponges, and soft coral in reds and orange. The water is famously clear, often well past 25 m, which is how the site earned its name. When the tide runs, grey reef and whitetip sharks hold in the flow on the up current side while clouds of fusiliers, snapper, and surgeonfish wrap the slopes and anthias swarm the shallows. Plate corals around 17 m shelter resting whitetip pups.
getThere: Fly into Labuan Bajo (LBJ / Komodo Airport) on Flores, which has multiple daily domestic flights from Bali (DPS) and Jakarta (CGK). Crystal Rock lies in the far north of the park near Gili Lawa, roughly 3 to 4 hours by fast day boat from Labuan Bajo harbour and most often dived as a stop on a Komodo liveaboard.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: The crown of the seamount sits in ripping current, and hooking into bare reef is the only way to hold position while the sharks and schooling fish stream past.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB and reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits put you over open water, so send a marker buoy up before you ascend.

notes: Negative entry and consistently strong current make this an advanced dive, not a spot for new divers. Surface on the down current side and never up current of the rock. Komodo National Park entry and ranger fees apply, and most divers reach the far north on a liveaboard rather than a day boat.

name: Balun
description: Balun is a wall dive on the open Adriatic flank of the northern Kornati islands, where the archipelago's limestone crowns drop from the surface into deep blue water. Finning southeast along the cliff, divers reach a terrace near 26 metres where the rock turns thick with red and violet gorgonian sea fans (Paramuricea clavata) and golden Eunicella, packed among yellow sulphur sponges at a density rarely seen elsewhere in the Mediterranean. Dusky groupers hang off the wall while conger eels, scorpionfish and spiny lobsters wedge into the cracks. Diving here is permitted only with a licensed centre inside Kornati National Park.

conditionsByMonth.suitRecommendation: 7mm wetsuit with hood; cooler below the thermocline
7mm wetsuit with hood; cooler below the thermocline
7mm wetsuit with hood; cooler below the thermocline
7mm wetsuit with hood; cooler below the thermocline

getThere: Fly into Zadar (ZAD) or Split (SPU), both served by seasonal European flights. From either, reach the harbour town of Murter, the main gateway to the park, and join a licensed dive boat for the crossing of roughly 19 nautical miles to the northern Kornati islands. Day boats and liveaboards also run from Zadar and Biograd.
notes: Diving inside Kornati National Park is allowed only through a licensed dive centre, in designated zones, and a daily park ticket is required. The richest gorgonian growth sits below 25 metres, so plan for cold water at depth and conservative no decompression limits.

name: Chandelier Cave
description: A flooded limestone sea cave in Malakal Harbor, a few minutes by boat from Koror, where five chambers run back into the rock and four of them trap pockets of breathable air. Divers drop through the entrance at about 4 m, follow the divemaster through shallow submerged passages, and surface inside the chambers to stand among stalactites and stalagmites that took tens of thousands of years to grow, fingers of rock hanging from the ceiling to meet columns rising off the floor. Depth stays under 15 m and the water is still, but the chambers are dark and silt clouds easily, so a torch and slow, controlled buoyancy matter. Out at the entrance wall, cardinalfish and soldierfish gather in the shadows, banded coral shrimp work the cracks, and mandarinfish appear around dusk.
getThere: Fly into Koror (ROR / Roman Tmetuchl International), Palau's only international airport, with connections from Manila, Taipei, Guam, and Tokyo. The cave sits in Malakal Harbor on the south side of Koror, a five to ten minute boat ride from town dive shops, so it is usually run as a second or third dive of the day rather than a dedicated trip.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: The inner chambers are an overhead environment with little natural light, so a torch is needed to see the stalactite formations and the way back out.

siteSpecificGear.name: Backup light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Standard redundancy for the overhead sections in case the primary torch floods or fails.

notes: Overhead environment: stay with the guide, carry a torch, and move slowly because the fine sediment clouds the chambers fast and the formations are fragile. No technical cave certification is required and depth stays shallow, but it suits divers comfortable with buoyancy. Sheltered harbor location means it dives year round regardless of swell. Requires the Koror State permit and Palau Pristine Paradise Environmental Fee. Often paired with a dusk mandarinfish dive on the nearby Malakal Harbor wall.

name: Japanese Gardens
description: A drift dive off the southern tip of Goat Island, where the Guyana Current pushes divers along a slope crowded with orange elephant ear sponges, yellow tube sponges and grey barrel sponges before funneling them through the Kamikaze Cut, a narrow gap that speeds up the flow. The same nutrient laden water that grows those sponges feeds hawksbill turtles, nurse sharks tucked under ledges, and schools of barracuda and tarpon, with manta rays passing through in the greener plankton months. The dive runs from a sponge covered shelf near the island down past 30 m.
getThere: Fly into A.N.R. Robinson International (TAB) at Crown Point in southwest Tobago, with frequent hops from Port of Spain on Trinidad and seasonal service from the UK and North America. Speyside sits on the northeast coast about 50 km away, a drive of roughly 1.5 hours along the windward coast road. Dive operators in Speyside Bay run boats to the south tip of Goat Island in about 10 minutes.
notes: Strong drift is both the draw and the hazard: the current accelerates through a gap the guides call the Kamikaze Cut, so expect a fast ride and carry an SMB for the live boat pickup. It dives best on a slack to building tide with a guide who knows the timing. Plankton heavy water from the Guyana Current keeps visibility variable and feeds the sponges. The site lies within northeast Tobago waters managed for reef protection, so spearfishing and glove use are discouraged.

name: Barren Island
description: India's only active volcano breaks the surface about 140 km northeast of Port Blair, and the reef ringing its base is unlike anywhere else in the Andamans — the sand here is black volcanic ash, not white, and old lava flows have set into stepped ledges, arches and walls that drop past 100 m. Reef manta rays are the headline: a group of up to nine works the cleaning stations off the western black sand bay, banking slowly while cleaner wrasse pick them over. Whitetip reef sharks patrol the drop offs, green and hawksbill turtles graze the shallows, and schools of needlefish, barracuda and trevally hang in water that routinely clears to 40 m or more. Sites such as the Washing Machine throw strong down currents, and with the island reachable only by liveaboard this stays firmly advanced ground.
getThere: Fly into Veer Savarkar International Airport (IXZ) at Port Blair via Chennai (MAA), Kolkata (CCU), Delhi (DEL) or Bengaluru (BLR). Barren Island lies about 140 km northeast of Port Blair and roughly 100 km from Havelock (Swaraj Dweep), with no jetty and no permitted landing, so it is reached only by liveaboard or a long private dive charter. The open ocean crossing runs about 4 to 5 hours from Port Blair or 2 to 3 hours from Havelock, and most divers join an organised multi day expedition rather than a day boat.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light or torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: Lava ledges, arches and the deeper walls fall into shadow, and a primary torch brings out the colour of the gorgonian fans and helps with shark and turtle ID.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nitrox cert + EAN32 fills
siteSpecificGear.reason: Manta cleaning stations and the lava reef sit deep enough that air bottom time is short; EAN32 stretches no deco limits and is worth arranging with the liveaboard before departure.

notes: Advanced Open Water with solid current experience is the working minimum; sites like the Washing Machine throw strong down currents. Landing on Barren Island is prohibited, it is an active volcano (last erupted 2017 to 2018) and a protected zone, so all diving is from boats with no shore access. The season runs roughly October to May; the southwest monsoon from June to September makes the offshore crossing unsafe. Trips run by liveaboard and book out well ahead because few vessels are permitted here. Keep clear of mantas on the cleaning stations and do not touch the gorgonian fans.

name: First Cathedral
description: A collapsed volcanic lava tube off Lanai's leeward south coast, southeast of Manele Bay, forms a cavern the size of a small chapel: roughly 30 m long and two stories tall, entered through a wide archway whose floor drops to about 18 m. Where the roof caved in, holes in the ceiling let shafts of sunlight pour onto a fallen block of basalt that divers call the Altar, and on calm summer middays the beams turn the chamber into a stained glass effect that gave the site its name. Walls crusted with orange cup coral shelter green sea turtles, resting whitetip reef sharks, spiny lobster wedged into cracks and clouds of endemic Hawaiian reef fish. The classic finish is the Shotgun, a narrow chimney at the back of the cavern where divers time the surge and get flushed into open water one at a time.
getThere: Fly into Kahului Airport (OGG) on Maui. First Cathedral is dived almost entirely from Maui: charter boats leave Lahaina and Maalaea harbors and cross the Auau Channel to Lanai's sheltered south coast in about an hour, with the calmest water on early morning departures. A few operators run from Manele Harbor on Lanai, reached by the Expeditions passenger ferry from Lahaina. The site sits in Maui County, not on the Kona Coast of the Big Island.
notes: This is a cavern dive in an overhead environment, entered through an archway and finished through a tight chimney called the Shotgun where divers ride the surge out one at a time, so most operators require Advanced Open Water or a confident certified diver with good buoyancy. Sand stirred off the floor clouds the chamber fast, so the first group in gets the cleanest light, and the sunbeams on the Altar are sharpest on calm summer middays near noon. Winter Kona storms and rougher channel crossings cancel trips at short notice.

name: Paradise Reef
description: Paradise Reef is the shallow coral strip just south of Cozumel's cruise piers and the northernmost reef inside the island's national marine park. Three low ridges of sponges and gorgonians sit in 9 to 15 m of gentle drift, which makes it the standard Cozumel checkout dive and its most reliable night dive. After dark, torch beams pick out the splendid toadfish, a striped electric blue fish that lives nowhere on Earth but Cozumel, wedged under ledges and croaking from its burrow. Spotted eagle rays pass through in winter while octopus, banded coral shrimp and snapper work the reef once the sun drops.
getThere: Cozumel (CZM) has direct flights from many US cities, or fly into Cancun (CUN) and take the 45 minute ferry from Playa del Carmen. Paradise Reef is a few minutes by boat from the San Miguel waterfront, just south of the cruise terminals.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: Paradise is dived mainly after dark, when a strong torch is what reveals the splendid toadfish under ledges.

siteSpecificGear.name: Surface marker buoy
siteSpecificGear.reason: The reef sits beside the cruise ship channel, so divers surface under an SMB clear of boat traffic.

notes: Inside Cozumel Reefs National Park, so a park fee or wristband applies. The site borders the cruise ship channel and dives are timed around heavy boat traffic. Night dives are the highlight.

name: Shark Ray Alley
description: Zone D of Hol Chan Marine Reserve, about a mile south of the reserve's main cut off Ambergris Caye. Decades of fish cleaning by local fishermen drew Atlantic nurse sharks and southern stingrays into the shallow sand patch, and now twenty or more sharks routinely circle in waist deep water as boats arrive. Depths run only 5 to 12 feet over a white sand bottom, so divers and snorkelers share the column with the animals. The draw is close encounters and natural light photography rather than reef structure, and most operators pair it with a Hol Chan reef dive on the same trip.
getThere: Fly into Philip S. W. Goldson International Airport (BZE) near Belize City, then take a 15 minute Tropic Air or Maya Island Air hop to San Pedro on Ambergris Caye (SPR). Most San Pedro dive operators run the trip as a half day boat charter combined with Hol Chan, roughly 20 minutes south of town.
notes: Inside Hol Chan Marine Reserve (Zone D), so a per person park fee applies and is usually bundled by operators. No gloves, no touching, no chasing. Sharks are habituated and generally calm but bites have occurred when divers grab them or get between them and bait. Often combined with a Hol Chan reef dive on the same boat trip. Suitable as a snorkel or for first dives; current is typically mild.

name: Aida II Wreck
description: A 75 m steel supply ship that struck Big Brother in storm winds on 15 September 1957 while offloading personnel for the island lighthouse. The hull broke in two: the engine block lies wedged on the reef at 7 m where the surf caught her, the bow rests against the wall at 30 m, the deck and railings run down past 50 m, and the propeller sits at 65 m for tech divers only. Soft corals and gorgonians now sheath every steel rib, clouds of glassfish boil through the holds, and grey reef sharks cruise the blue off her stern. She lies a few hundred metres along the same north reef as the Numidia and is almost always run as the second dive of the day.
getThere: Liveaboard only, departing Hurghada (HRG) or Marsa Alam (RMF) with direct European flights. Southern Red Sea itineraries taking in Brothers, Daedalus and Elphinstone run 5 to 7 nights and reach the El Ikhwa (Brother) Islands offshore in the central Red Sea.
notes: Brothers Marine Park is liveaboard only and generally accessible from March to October. The Aida descends a near vertical wall: engine block at 7 m, bow at 30 m, midship past 50 m and propeller at 65 m, so the lower hull is technical or trimix only. Strong, unpredictable currents off the point demand reef hooks and SMBs.

name: Nudi Falls (Lembeh Strait)
description: Mainland side mark in the middle of the Lembeh Strait, just north of Tandu Rusa village. The dive starts shallow on a soft coral encrusted wall that drops to about 15 m, then transitions to a rubble and sand slope feeding a broad field of large soft corals from 20 m down. The site earned its name because divers' exhaust bubbles dislodge nudibranchs from the wall above, sending tiny slugs raining down through the water column. It is also the most reliable spot in the strait for the Lembeh seadragon, Pontoh's pygmy seahorse, harlequin shrimp, rhinopias scorpionfish, wonderpus and blue ringed octopus, and ornate ghost pipefish. Currents are typically calm.
getThere: Fly into Sam Ratulangi International (MDC) in Manado, North Sulawesi. From the airport it is a 90 minute road transfer to Bitung port, then a short crossing by traditional outrigger or resort speedboat to one of the Lembeh resorts. Nudi Falls sits in the middle of the strait on the mainland side, roughly a 10 minute boat ride from most resort jetties.
notes: Classic muck and critter site. Bring a focus light, a pointer, and a macro lens (60 to 105 mm). Buoyancy discipline is essential: the sand below the wall holds seadragons and pygmy pipehorses that one careless fin kick can bury. The wall itself is decorated with delicate soft corals and crinoids that should never be touched. Best diving in dry season (March through October) when visibility improves, though macro life is rich year round.

name: Cara a Cara
description: Roatan's signature shark dive on a 21 m sand plateau off the south coast near Cordelia Banks. A resident group of 10 to 15 Caribbean reef sharks, almost all mature females reaching 2 to 3 m, circles a baited drum while divers kneel in a half ring on the sand. The reef edge drops off behind the action and the closing passes bring the animals within arm's length. Current can rip across the plateau between sets.
getThere: Fly into Roatan (RTB / Juan Manuel Galvez International) which has direct flights from several US hubs plus connections via San Pedro Sula (SAP). The dive runs as a half day boat trip out of West End, West Bay, or French Cay marinas; expect 25 to 35 minutes of transit each way.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Current can push divers off the sand circle between bait passes; a hook lets you hold position without scraping the bottom.

notes: Baited shark dive run under a permitted operator. Divers stay in a stationary semicircle on the sand at 21 m while staff present a bait drum; do not chase the animals. The sharks are habituated and have produced no recorded recreational fatalities, but a baited environment is not without risk. Advanced certification and a recent dive log are typically required.

name: Steve's Bommie
description: An isolated coral tower on Ribbon Reef Number 3 that climbs from a 30 m sand floor to within 5 m of the surface. Schools of bigeye trevally, fusiliers and yellow-lined snapper rotate around the pinnacle in dense columns while macro hunters search the crevices for leafy scorpionfish, stonefish, longnose hawkfish and peacock mantis shrimp. Anemonefish defend host anemones near the summit and grey, whitetip and blacktip reef sharks patrol the deeper edges. A memorial plaque to Steve, the free diver the site is named after, sits near the base at about 25 m.
getThere: Fly into Cairns Airport (CNS). Steve's Bommie sits on Ribbon Reef 3, roughly 240 km north of Cairns and well past day boat range, so access is by liveaboard. Three to seven night Ribbon Reefs and Coral Sea itineraries depart Cairns weekly with Mike Ball Dive Expeditions (Spoilsport), Spirit of Freedom and Divers Den OceanQuest; northbound trips often return by light aircraft from Lizard Island to Cairns.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro lens or torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The pinnacle is a top critter dive on the Ribbon Reefs; leafy scorpionfish, stonefish and longnose hawkfish reward a close inspection lens or pocket torch.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer with logbook room
siteSpecificGear.reason: A typical liveaboard schedule stacks four to five dives a day; surface intervals on the boat are short and Steve's is often dived twice on the same itinerary.

notes: Marine park fees apply and are usually included in liveaboard pricing. Stonefish at this site are camouflaged on the pinnacle wall; do not hand hold on the reef and watch fin placement near the base. Currents are usually mild but can pick up on a turning tide, in which case the lee side of the bommie offers shelter and concentrates the fish schools.

name: Cenote Carwash
description: Roadside cenote 8 km west of Tulum on the highway toward Cobá, named for taxi drivers who once rinsed their cabs in the open pool. Lily pads and water plants carpet the surface; in the rainy season the canopy filters sunlight into emerald shafts that rake the cavern floor. Divers enter through a sunlit cavern past stalactites, cross a halocline near 14 m, and follow a marked line into the cave system beyond the daylight zone. Freshwater turtles patrol the shallows, schools of mojarra dart through the beams, and a small crocodile sometimes rests beneath the lilies.
getThere: Fly into Cancún (CUN) and drive about 1.5 hours south on Highway 307 to Tulum, then turn inland on Highway 109 toward Cobá. The cenote entrance sits on the right about 8 km in, a few hundred metres past Cenote Zacil-Ha. Access is a short walk from the parking area down steps to an open pool; most Tulum and Playa del Carmen shops run it as part of a two-cenote guided trip.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cavern interior darkens off the line and the back of the room sees little surface light.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Repeated halocline crossings near 14 m stack up bottom time across a multi cenote day.

notes: A cavern guide is required for the daylight zone; only fully cave certified divers should follow the line past the air dome into the deeper system. Emerald light beams are strongest from May to October when the surface algae carpet thickens; the dry season gives clearer water but a different blue cast. A resident juvenile Morelet's crocodile is sometimes seen near the lilies — give it space and do not approach.

name: SS Rosalie Moller
description: British coal freighter sunk in the early hours of 8 October 1941 by two Luftwaffe Heinkel He 111s, two days after the same bombers claimed her famous sister wreck SS Thistlegorm just 15 km away. Launched in Glasgow in 1910 as the Francis, the 108 metre hull sat hidden for nearly six decades until divers relocated her in 1998. She rests upright on a sandy seabed in the Strait of Gubal: masts at 18 m, main deck at 35 m, propeller and rudder at 50 m, holds still packed with the Welsh steam coal she was carrying to Alexandria. Silt off the surrounding sand keeps visibility around 10 to 20 m, lending the wreck a brooding atmosphere lit by glassfish swarms over bridge and engine room while batfish, schools of barracuda and resident grouper patrol the masts.

conditionsByMonth.suitRecommendation: 5mm wetsuit with hood or drysuit for tech profiles
5mm wetsuit with hood or drysuit for tech profiles

getThere: Fly to Hurghada International (HRG) on the Egyptian mainland, roughly 30 km southwest of the wreck across the Strait of Gubal — the closest and most common approach. Day boats run from Hurghada and El Gouna marinas, a 90 minute speedboat transfer each way, while most divers prefer to visit on a 3 to 7 night northern Red Sea liveaboard out of Hurghada that pairs the Rosalie Moller with the SS Thistlegorm, Sha'ab Abu Nuhas and Ras Mohammed. Sharm el Sheikh (SSH) day trips are possible by speedboat but the longer crossing is rarely worth it.
notes: Technical certification recommended: the propeller and stern sit at 50 m and the main deck at 35 m, putting most of the wreck below recreational limits. Many operators run it as an extended range or trimix dive; some allow accompanied dives on nitrox with strict 40 m caps over the bridge and forward holds only. Two primary plus a backup torch are essential — the holds are dark, the silt is fine and easily stirred, and a thermocline often hangs around 22 m. Currents are usually gentle but the surface around Gubal can pick up suddenly; descend and ascend on the permanent mooring line. The wreck is a war grave for the two crew lost in 1941. Avoid touching cargo or hull plating, much of which is degrading rapidly. Nearest hyperbaric chamber is in Hurghada.

name: Yamagiri Maru
description: A 134 m Japanese passenger cargo ship sunk on the second day of Operation Hailstone in February 1944 by SBD Dauntless bombers from USS Yorktown and USS Bunker Hill. She rests on her port side a mile north north west of Fefan Island, the hull stretching from 12 m at the starboard rail to 34 m on the sand. Hold five still cradles stacks of 18 inch armour piercing shells cast for the battleship Musashi, their copper driving bands green with patina. The engine room and bridge are open for trained penetration divers, the props and rudder buried under three quarters of a century of black coral and crinoids. Human remains and live ordnance are still inside the hull.
getThere: Fly to Chuuk International (TKK) on Weno via United's Guam (GUM) or Honolulu (HNL) island hopper, or via Pohnpei (PNI). From Weno it is a 30 to 40 minute boat ride south to the wreck off Fefan Island; almost every dive operation on the lagoon visits Yamagiri Maru on a standard rotation.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Primary plus backup torches for hold five and engine room penetration; ambient light fades fast on the port side resting wreck.

siteSpecificGear.name: Computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multi level deep wreck near the 30 m bracket; nitrox extends bottom time on the holds.

notes: Wreck certification required for any interior penetration. Live 18 inch armour piercing shells are stacked in hold five and other ordnance is scattered throughout the holds; touch nothing. The wreck is a war grave for Japanese crew members lost in February 1944. Silt outs come fast when finning over the lower port side hull, so trim and buoyancy control matter.

name: The Canyon (Dahab)
description: A limestone slot canyon punched through the fringing reef 8 km north of Dahab, entered through a shallow sandy lagoon called the Fish Bowl at 14 m. The fissure snakes upward from the depths almost closed at the top, with three windows in the roof: the Fish Bowl entry, a wide opening at 22 m, and the seaward exit at 52 m where divers swim out into open water. Inside, glassfish swarm the chimney walls in clouds thick enough to dim the surface light. The coral garden outside at 5 to 10 m holds octopus, scorpionfish, lionfish and bluespotted ribbontail rays, and serves as both the safety stop and the recreational alternative for divers staying out of the slot itself.
getThere: Fly into Sharm el-Sheikh International (SSH), then drive about 1.5 hours north up the coast to Dahab. The Canyon car park sits roughly 8 km north of town along a rough coastal track, usually reached by 4x4 or pickup arranged through any local dive centre. Walk across the shingle beach and a short rocky platform to enter the Fish Bowl lagoon directly from shore. Taba (TCP) and a longer transfer from Hurghada (HRG) via the Sinai are alternative airports.

siteSpecificGear.name: Torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The canyon roof is almost closed at the top; a light reveals the glassfish curtains and the cracks in the limestone walls

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer with deep alarms
siteSpecificGear.reason: The exit at 52 m sits well outside recreational limits and narcosis at depth has killed divers here

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Surface support is shore based and boat traffic crosses the bay; mark your ascent if you drift away from the entry

notes: Overhead environment with all canyon depths exceeding recreational limits and a documented history of narcosis fatalities at the deep exit. Many divers and operators treat the slot as a technical objective and run the shallow coral garden plus the Fish Bowl as a separate open water profile. Shore access only; a small fee covers the Bedouin car park and rest area at the entry.

name: Lesleen M Wreck
description: A 50 metre cargo freighter sunk by the Saint Lucia Department of Fisheries in October 1986 as the country's first purpose laid artificial reef, just off Anse Cochon on the leeward coast. The Lesleen M sits fully upright on a flat sand floor, wheelhouse rising to roughly 12 m and keel at 20 m, with the holds, engine room, and bridge open and easily swum through for divers with basic wreck training. Four decades of growth have wrapped the hull in encrusting sponges, fire coral, and small gorgonians; resident green moray eels coil in the engine room, schools of horse eye jacks circle the bridge, and frogfish occasionally settle on the rails. Shallow enough for two long, calm dives back to back.
getThere: Hewanorra International (UVF) at the south of Saint Lucia is the closest major airport, about 60 minutes by road to the dive resorts along the west coast; George F. L. Charles (SLU) near Castries handles only regional flights but is 25 minutes from operators in the north. The wreck is boat only, dived from operators based at Marigot Bay, Anse Chastanet, or Soufrière — typical run is 10 to 20 minutes along the leeward coast.
notes: Sits inside the Canaries Marine Management Area; permit fees are collected by operators. The interior is well lit through hatch openings, with no overhead silt traps in the main holds, so most dive shops run a guided swim through on a standard open water certification — basic wreck training is recommended but not always required. A torch is still useful for the engine room and crew quarters. Mooring buoys mark the bow and stern; do not anchor. The Vicky B, a smaller tug sunk in 2003, lies 200 m to the south and is often added as a second tank by operators staying on site.

name: HTMS Sattakut
description: Ex Royal Thai Navy infantry landing craft scuttled in June 2011 about 800 metres southwest of Koh Tao as a wreck specialty training site. The Sattakut sits upright on a sandy plain with the seabed at 30 metres and the deck around 18 to 22 metres. The bow and stern gun mounts remain in place and are the signature photo subjects, drawing schools of yellowtail fusiliers and snapper that hover in the lee of the railings. Penetration of the wheelhouse and cargo holds is possible for divers with wreck training; the engine room is reserved for more experienced wreck divers carrying lights. Originally USS LCI(L) 742, the ship served at Iwo Jima before its 1947 transfer to the Royal Thai Navy and 2011 sinking.
getThere: Fly to Bangkok (BKK or DMK), then transfer to Chumphon airport (CJM) or Surat Thani (URT). From Chumphon take the Lomprayah catamaran to Koh Tao (about 1.5 hours); from Surat Thani via Donsak ferry pier (around 3 hours). Dive shops in Mae Haad and Sairee Beach run boats to the wreck, a 20 to 30 minute ride.
notes: Use the mooring buoys; the wreck sits on sand and is easy to lose in poorer visibility. Wreck specialty certification is required for penetration of the wheelhouse and cargo holds. The deeper engine room and crew quarters need a torch and are typically reserved for divers with technical or advanced wreck training. The site is often combined with Hin Pee Wee pinnacle 30 metres to the northwest as a multi level dive. Visibility drops sharply during the November monsoon when many operators skip the site.

name: Third Encounter
description: A horseshoe shaped volcanic seamount about half a mile off Saba's western coast, rising from over 600 m of open ocean to a plateau at 29 m. The signature feature is Eye of the Needle, a free standing pinnacle whose summit lifts to within 27 m of the surface, ringed by horse eye jacks and patrolled by Caribbean reef sharks. The plateau hosts Nassau groupers, nurse sharks, and creole wrasse, with one long resident grouper known to divers as Charlie; deeper edges drop into blue water where manta rays and the occasional pelagic pass through. Plankton rich water and moderate current are typical, and the site has been voted among the Caribbean's top pinnacle dives.
getThere: St. Maarten (SXM / Princess Juliana International) has direct flights from North America, Europe, and across the Caribbean. Winair operates the short hop to Saba (SAB), or you can take the ~90 minute ferry to Fort Bay. Dive boats reach Third Encounter in about 20 to 25 minutes from Fort Bay harbour.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Exposed seamount with moderate current; drift exits common.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer
siteSpecificGear.reason: Multi level pinnacle profile with deep portions.

notes: Saba Marine Park access fee is collected by operators. Depth and exposure make this an advanced level dive and nitrox is commonly used to extend bottom time. Sharks are unbaited. Hurricane season is June through November though sustained storm impact on Saba is uncommon.

name: Iro Maru
description: A 143 m Imperial Japanese Navy fleet oiler torpedoed by USS Tunny and finished off by US carrier aircraft during Operation Desecrate One in March 1944, sitting upright in 40 m of water in the Urukthapel anchorage south of Koror. The signature feature is the bow gun, a deck cannon on its circular platform around 27 m, smothered in soft coral and sponges with anemonefish in the tendrils; an identical aft gun sits past the loading kingposts. Masts rise to 8 m below the surface and make a long descent line down to the main deck at 25 m. Penetration of cargo holds and bridge is open to divers with wreck training. Glassfish school in the holds, lionfish stake out the kingposts, and batfish patrol the deck.
getThere: Fly into Koror (ROR / Roman Tmetuchl International) with connections from Manila, Taipei, Guam, and Tokyo. The wreck lies in the Urukthapel anchorage southeast of Koror, roughly a 15 minute boat ride from town dive shops, and is usually combined with a reef or channel dive on the same boat day.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: The cargo holds and engine room are dark; a torch is essential for inspecting the bow gun details and any interior penetration.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: A surface marker buoy is needed for the open water ascent off the mast top in the busy Koror boat lane.

notes: Live ordnance, including unexploded shells and small arms ammunition, remains on the wreck and inside cargo holds. Do not touch or move anything. Maximum depth of 40 m and silt bottom make this an advanced or nitrox dive; penetration of holds and bridge requires formal wreck training. Requires the Koror State permit and Palau Pristine Paradise Environmental Fee.

name: Eden Rock & Devil's Grotto
description: Twin limestone formations off George Town's South Church Street that rise from a sand floor to within a metre of the surface, riddled with caverns, tunnels and skylight chambers where shafts of sunlight cut through the dark interior. In summer a dense silver curtain of silversides packs the chambers, hunted by resident Atlantic tarpon in lunging attacks that scatter the schools into swirling clouds. Caribbean reef squid hover at the entrances and great barracuda patrol the perimeter, all in clear water that rarely runs more than a gentle drift. Depths of 4 to 15 m make this the most dived shore site on Grand Cayman.
getThere: Fly into Owen Roberts International Airport (GCM) on Grand Cayman. The site sits directly off South Church Street in George Town, roughly 400 m south of the cruise piers. Most divers shore enter from Eden Rock Diving Center's purpose built ladder or by boat from any Seven Mile Beach operator; the swim from shore to the formations is about 100 m.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: The caverns and tunnel chambers go dark even at midday; a torch lets you see structure and pick out silversides packed into the ceiling pockets.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Cruise ship tenders and snorkel boats work the surface above the site through the day; a marker is essential before ascent.

notes: Inside the George Town port zone; check the daily cruise ship schedule before diving, as boat traffic above the site is heavy when ships are berthed. The silversides aggregation is a summer phenomenon (roughly June through September) and is the reason most divers pick this site over Cayman's offshore walls. Avoid touching or chasing the tarpon, which feed naturally through the schools.

name: Antilla Wreck
description: A 122 m German cargo ship scuttled by her own crew on 10 May 1940 to avoid Dutch capture after Germany invaded the Netherlands. The hull lies on its port side off Malmok Bay with the top deck at 5 m and the seabed at 18 m, broken amidships by decades of storms. Tube sponges, soft coral, and orange cup coral encrust the steel; hawksbill turtles, green morays, blue tang, and lobsters work the holds. Snorkelers reach the upper rails while divers swim the cargo bays and crew quarters.
getThere: Queen Beatrix International (AUA) sits on the south side of the island with direct flights from major US East Coast hubs, Toronto, Amsterdam, and several Latin American cities. Dive operators run the boat trip from Oranjestad or Palm Beach to the wreck in roughly 20 to 30 minutes.
notes: Storm damage has continued to break up the hull since 1953; some operators no longer allow penetration. Surface conditions can get choppy when the trade winds pick up in winter.

name: Wattabomi
description: Coral bommie field on the south flank of Mnemba Atoll, sloping gently from 6m to 30m through a labyrinth of massive hard coral blocks. The site name is a play on what a bommie, and the bommies themselves are the draw. Bottlenose dolphins cruise the shallows throughout the year and often pass through the reef during a dive. Between the coral blocks, crevices shelter stonefish, scorpionfish, leaf fish, frogfish, octopus, and snowflake moray; macro hunters work the coral fingers for nudibranchs and porcelain crabs in anemones. Currents stay mild, so the dive runs as a slow drift suitable for all skill levels.
getThere: Fly into Abeid Amani Karume International Airport (ZNZ) on Unguja. Most operators run from Matemwe or Nungwi on the northeast coast (60 to 90 minutes by road from the airport); from there it is a 15 to 30 minute dhow or speedboat crossing to the atoll. Day trips also leave from Stone Town and Kendwa with longer transit times.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro lens or wet diopter
siteSpecificGear.reason: Reputation here rests on small subjects (frogfish, leaf fish, nudibranchs, porcelain crabs) hiding between coral blocks; a macro setup is the difference between seeing them and missing them.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Most of the dive is a drift, but pockets between bommies let you pause if you want to work a single subject; a hook holds position without touching coral.

notes: Inside the Mnemba Island Marine Conservation Area (MIMCA) — a daily marine park fee is collected by operators and is usually not included in the dive price. The island itself is private (andBeyond Mnemba Island lodge); landings are not permitted for day visitors, but the surrounding reefs are open to permitted dive boats. Touching coral and chasing dolphins are prohibited. Stonefish and scorpionfish are common between the bommies, so stay off the bottom and keep buoyancy tight.

name: Admiralty Anchor (Osprey Reef)
description: A wall site on Osprey Reef's western edge named for a centuries old admiralty pattern anchor wedged inside a narrow cave passage at around 20 m. The coral terrace at 8 to 15 m steps off into a vertical wall that vanishes into more than 1,000 m of indigo Coral Sea water. Nutrient rich currents rise against the structure and pull in grey reef and whitetip sharks, schools of midnight snapper, red tail wrasses, and lyretail hogfish; eagle rays and the occasional dwarf nautilus drift past the deeper edges. Visibility routinely tops 60 m and can exceed 80 m from September to November when the trade winds drop.
getThere: Cairns is the nearest international airport. Osprey Reef sits about 350 km offshore in the Coral Sea and is reached only by dedicated three to seven day liveaboard expeditions from Cairns or Port Douglas. Operators run scheduled trips year round, with calmest seas and clearest water from September through November.
notes: Remote Coral Sea site reached only by liveaboard. The cave passage is narrow and requires good buoyancy and a torch. Liveaboard departures from Cairns can be limited during the January to February cyclone window.

name: Sha'ab Sataya (Dolphin House Fury Shoals)
description: Largest reef of the Fury Shoals complex, a 4.7 km horseshoe lying 15 km off Hamata in the deep south of the Egyptian Red Sea. The northern lagoon shelters a resident pod of roughly 100 to 200 spinner dolphins (Stenella longirostris) that return each morning to rest, court and nurse calves after feeding offshore overnight. Snorkelers share the calm lagoon with the pod under marine reserve rules, while scuba dives explore the outer crescent: hard coral plateaus at 5 to 12 m roll over into walls and dropoffs to 40 m, broken by swimthroughs, pinnacles and gardens of Dendronephthya soft coral. Fusiliers, jacks, Spanish dancers, hawksbill turtles and whitetip reef sharks patrol the outer wall. Currents stay mild inside the horseshoe and stiffen on the southern tip.
getThere: Fly into Marsa Alam International (RMF). Sataya sits at the southern edge of the Fury Shoals about 15 km off Hamata marina, roughly a 2 hour transfer south from Marsa Alam by road then 60 to 90 minutes by speedboat. Most divers reach it on southern Red Sea liveaboards (Hurghada, Port Ghalib or Marsa Alam departures) on 4 to 7 day Fury Shoals and Saint Johns itineraries; daytrip boats based in Hamata also run snorkel and dive trips when weather allows.

siteSpecificGear.name: Snorkel set
siteSpecificGear.reason: Dolphin encounters happen in the shallow lagoon without scuba, so bring mask, fins and snorkel for time in the water with the pod

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Outer reef dives often finish as drifts on the southern plateau, and boat traffic in the lagoon is heavy

notes: Sataya lies inside Egypt's Red Sea marine reserve network and operators are expected to follow the same dolphin code as at Samadai: keep distance, do not chase, touch or feed the pod, no flash photography, and stay out of the inner resting zone. Best dolphin contact is early morning before snorkel boats arrive. The outer reef is exposed to northerly swell in winter and currents can stiffen on the south plateau. Liveaboard or daytrip boat only, no shore access.

name: Boo Windows
description: Largest reef of the Fury Shoals complex, a 4.7 km horseshoe lying 15 km off Hamata in the deep south of the Egyptian Red Sea. The northern lagoon shelters a resident pod of roughly 100 to 200 spinner dolphins (Stenella longirostris) that return each morning to rest, court and nurse calves after feeding offshore overnight. Snorkelers share the calm lagoon with the pod under protected-area rules, while scuba dives explore the outer crescent: hard coral plateaus at 5 to 12 m roll over into walls and drop-offs to 40 m, broken by swim-throughs, pinnacles and gardens of Dendronephthya soft coral. Fusiliers, jacks, Spanish dancers, hawksbill turtles and whitetip reef sharks patrol the outer wall. Currents are mild inside the horseshoe and stronger on the south-facing tip.
getThere: Fly to Sorong (SOQ) in West Papua via Jakarta, Makassar, or Manado. From Sorong the only practical access to Misool is by liveaboard or by chartered speedboat to Misool Eco Resort; the run south takes most of a day and most operators sail overnight.
notes: Marine reserve fee for Raja Ampat is required (tag purchased in Sorong or Waisai). Currents on the southwest face can run strong; entry timing matters. Wobbegongs camouflage under coral plates — do not place hands.

name: Painted Walls
description: On the west side of Dead Chest Island in the British Virgin Islands, four narrow gullies cut into the limestone to form parallel canyons whose vertical walls are sheeted in encrusting cup corals in yellow, red, orange, and purple, with rope and finger sponges threaded through the cracks. Pillar corals stand in the sandy gaps between ridges. A swim through arch on the northern ridge opens into a small basin where silversides ball up and resident tarpon hang at the entrance. Hawksbill turtles graze the slopes, nurse sharks rest beneath ledges, and green moray eels watch from holes in the wall. Currents are typically low and the profile bottoms near 18 m, so the colour and texture of the walls reward a slow recreational pace.
getThere: Fly to Terrance B. Lettsome International (EIS) on Beef Island, Tortola, or arrive by ferry from St. Thomas (STT) in the US Virgin Islands. Dive boats from Road Town and Cooper Island reach the moorings on the west side of Dead Chest Island in about 20 minutes; the site is usually paired as a second tank with the nearby RMS Rhone.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The swim through arch and the deep cracks in the wall corals are dark; a torch reveals the layered cup coral colour and the silversides packed into the basin.

notes: Painted Walls sits inside the BVI National Parks system; coral and marine life are protected. The arch is wide enough for an Open Water diver in good trim and is short, so penetration cert is not required. Hurricane season (August through October) brings reduced visibility and occasional closures.

name: Bari Reef
description: Shore dive fronting Sand Dollar Resort north of Kralendijk and the standing biodiversity champion of the Caribbean: over 420 fish species logged by REEF surveyors, more than any single site in the basin. The slope drops from 9 m to a sandy bottom around 30 m, with collapsed pier debris in the shallows that schools of grunts, goatfish, and sergeant majors use for cover. Reliable for the species hunt list: yellowhead jawfish brooding eggs, juvenile drums, seahorses on the sponges, and the longnose batfish and frogfish that draw macro shooters back week after week. Calm, current free, and divable straight off the beach 24 hours.
getThere: Fly into Bonaire (BON / Flamingo International) with direct connections from Houston, Newark, Atlanta, Miami, and Amsterdam. Bari Reef is a 5 minute drive north of Kralendijk along Kaya Gobernador Debrot, accessed from the public beach beside Sand Dollar Resort.

siteSpecificGear.name: Macro focus light
siteSpecificGear.reason: Frogfish, seahorses, and brooding jawfish on this reef are notoriously well camouflaged and reward a focused beam.

notes: Bari Reef sits inside Bonaire National Marine Park; every diver must purchase a Nature Tag fee before getting wet. Entry is straight off the sand at Isidel Beach Park. Boat traffic is constant near Sand Dollar and Den Laman piers, so a surface marker buoy is strongly advised on ascent.

name: HMS Russell
description: Royal Navy Duncan class battleship that struck two mines laid by German submarine U73 on 27 April 1916 and now rests upside down on sand 6 km east of Fort St Elmo off the Delimara peninsula. The 132 m hull sits at 115 m with the keel rising to roughly 100 m and the stern torn off by the second detonation. Discovered in 2003 and opened to permit holders in May 2019, the wreck is the largest tech accessible WWI battleship in the Mediterranean. Trimix teams tie shot lines to the inverted keel, drop alongside 12 inch gun barrels still in their barbettes, and pass open hatches where John Dory and forkbeard hold station inside the casemates. Visibility at depth regularly exceeds 30 m and the steel is draped in white gorgonians and red sponges.
getThere: Fly to Malta International Airport (MLA). Tech operators in Marsascala, Valletta and St Julian's run the wreck by RHIB; transit is roughly 30 minutes from Marsascala. Boat dive only; no shore access.
notes: Heritage Malta permit required for every dive; operators handle the paperwork. Trimix and full deco capability mandatory; runtimes are typically 90 to 120 minutes including decompression. Wreck is a protected war grave so no penetration and no artefact removal.

name: The Passage
description: A 1.6 km tidal channel separating Gam and Waigeo islands, narrow enough at points to throw a rope across. Limestone karst walls draped in orange soft corals plunge into water stained brown by mangrove tannins; archerfish hunt insects above the surface while wobbegongs sleep in cracks below. Tidal flow rips through on incoming and outgoing tide, so divers ride drifts past swimthroughs, undercut walls, and pygmy seahorses (Hippocampus denise and bargibanti) on sea fans. Light drops after midday as low sun and steep walls darken the channel.
getThere: Fly to Sorong (SOQ) via Jakarta, Makassar, or Manado. Ferry from Sorong to Waisai on Waigeo takes about 2 hours. The Passage sits at the western mouth of Kabui Bay and is reached by liveaboard or speedboat from homestays on Gam, Mansuar, or Waisai (45 to 90 minutes).
notes: Dive on slack or with the current rather than against it; rips can exceed 4 knots through the narrowest sections. Marine Park entry tag (about IDR 1,000,000 / USD 65 for foreign divers, valid one year) must be purchased in Sorong or Waisai before diving.

name: Kudarah Thila
description: Marine protected pinnacle in South Ari Atoll, gazetted in 1995. Top of the thila sits at 14 m and drops past 30 m, with two enormous overhangs on the east and west sides smothered in red and orange Dendronephthya soft corals, plus a swim through arch hung with sea fans. Five lined snapper and bluestripe snapper sit motionless in dense ledges while jacks, dogtooth tuna, and rainbow runners hunt the corners. Grey reef sharks and Napoleon wrasse work the deeper edges in current.
getThere: Fly into Velana International (MLE) in Malé. South Ari Atoll is reached by 25 to 30 minute domestic flight to Maamigili (DMM), then a short speedboat hop, or by a 90 minute speedboat / seaplane transfer from Malé. Most operators run Kudarah Thila as a midday boat dive from resorts on Kudarah, Angaga, Dhigurah, or Mahibadhoo.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents sweep over the top of the thila and divers commonly hook in at the upstream edge to hold position while the action passes.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: The signature overhangs and arch are deep cuts in the reef that go dark fast; a torch reveals the soft coral colour and is needed for the swim through.

notes: Marine protected area since 1995; collection of any marine life and anchoring on the thila are prohibited. Dive on incoming tide for best visibility and shark activity. Site is small and easily congested when multiple boats arrive together.

name: The Bells (Dahab)
description: An open chimney that drops through the fringing reef 250 m north of the Blue Hole, named for the chime steel tanks make against the limestone walls as divers thread the shaft one at a time. Entry is a small fissure in the reef plate; the chimney narrows and widens before opening onto a sheer wall at about 28 m, thick with red whip corals, gorgonians and clouds of lyretail anthias. From the exit most divers cruise south along the outer reef and recross the Saddle at 7 m to drop into the Blue Hole for the swim back to shore. Schooling yellowfin tuna and the occasional bigeye trevally pass in the blue off the wall; close in, glassfish swarm the cracks and bluespotted ribbontail rays rest on rubble shelves.
getThere: Fly into Sharm el Sheikh International (SSH) and drive about 1.5 hours north along the coast to Dahab. The Bells car park sits roughly 10 km north of town along a rough coastal track, usually reached by 4x4 or pickup arranged with any Dahab dive centre. From the parking shelves, walk down to the reef plate; the small entry fissure is on the seaward side, 250 m north of the Blue Hole shore entry. Taba (TCP) and a longer transfer from Hurghada (HRG) via the Sinai are alternative airports.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: The profile ends on the open wall before the Saddle and boat traffic crosses the Blue Hole channel; mark your ascent before you come up.

siteSpecificGear.name: Torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: Light reveals the soft coral colour inside the chimney and shows up lionfish and morays tucked into the wall cracks.

siteSpecificGear.name: Dive computer with depth alarm
siteSpecificGear.reason: The wall outside the chimney exit drops well past 100 m; an alarm keeps you on the planned 28 m exit ledge instead of the deep slope.

notes: Dived one diver at a time through the chimney to avoid silt outs and tank clatter against the walls. Standard profile is a one way drift south from the Bells exit, across the Saddle at 7 m, and into the Blue Hole for the swim back to shore. No boat access; entry is from the Bedouin parking shelves and exit is up the Blue Hole steps. Small site fee at the camps covers parking and the rest area.

name: Fish Rock Cave
description: Only true ocean cave dive in Australia — a 125 metre tunnel that punches straight through a rocky islet two kilometres southeast of Smoky Cape. The deep entrance opens at 24 m and the cave climbs through a chimney to a sunlit shallow chamber at 12 m, the so called Aquarium, fringed in pink gorgonian fans. Critically endangered grey nurse sharks aggregate around the rock all year, peaking from May through September when dozens stack in the deep gutters and rest at the cave mouth. Schooling hammerheads pass through from October to January. Resident black cod, wobbegongs, and giant cuttlefish work the walls; bull rays and turtles cruise the sand.
getThere: Closest airports are Port Macquarie (PQQ) about 1.5 hours south and Coffs Harbour (CFS) about 2 hours north, both with daily flights from Sydney. From either airport drive to South West Rocks on the NSW Mid North Coast. Fish Rock sits 2 km southeast of Smoky Cape and is boat access only — local operators run two tank charters from Trial Bay.

siteSpecificGear.name: Primary dive light
siteSpecificGear.reason: The mid section of the cave between the chimney and the deep entrance is fully dark.

siteSpecificGear.name: Backup torch
siteSpecificGear.reason: Mandatory for the through cave traverse; light failure inside the tunnel means turning the dive.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook or SMB
siteSpecificGear.reason: Currents around the rock can pick up sharply and surface marker buoys are essential for the open water ascent off the wall.

siteSpecificGear.name: 7 mm wetsuit
siteSpecificGear.reason: Peak grey nurse season sits between 17 and 20 C with long bottom times in the cave.

notes: Advanced Open Water or 15 plus logged dives required. Cave traverse should be done one diver at a time through the narrow chimney to avoid silt. Always enter at the deep end and exit through the shallow chamber. The site lies inside the Fish Rock Aggregation Critical Habitat for grey nurse sharks — no touching, no chasing, no flash photography on resting sharks.

name: Ennerdale Wreck
description: Royal Fleet Auxiliary tanker, 211 meters long, lost on 1 June 1970 when she struck uncharted granite pinnacles off Mammelles Islet at 07:37 local time. With her engine room flooding and fuel oil pouring into the Indian Ocean, Royal Navy divers placed scuttling charges and helicopters lifted them clear seconds before detonation, sinking her on the spot to keep the slick off Mahé. The hull broke into large sections that lie in 24 to 30 meters across white sand, bow and stern still recognisable. Around the kingposts and inside the open holds you find resident giant moray eels, schools of oriental sweetlips and yellow snapper packed under overhangs, batfish drifting in pairs, eagle rays cruising past, and the occasional tawny nurse shark on the sand. The largest wreck in Seychelles waters.
getThere: Fly to Seychelles International Airport (SEZ) on Mahé. The wreck sits roughly 12 km northeast of Victoria, off the island's northeast coast near Mammelles Islet, reached by a 30 to 45 minute boat run from dive centres at Beau Vallon, Bel Ombre or Victoria Marina. Most operators run it as a single deep dive in calm sea, often paired with a shallower second dive at Brissare Rocks or the Twin Barges.
notes: Deep dive on a fragmented wreck. PADI Deep Diver or equivalent certification expected, and most operators want 30 plus logged dives. Nitrox extends bottom time. The site is exposed and the run out can be rough in the southeast monsoon (May to September), so trips often cancel last minute in those months. Penetration of the broken sections is not recommended due to collapsed plating and sharp steel. Nearest hyperbaric chamber is at Seychelles Hospital in Victoria.

name: Pedernales Wreck
description: A Lake Maracaibo crude tanker torpedoed by a German submarine on the morning of 16 February 1942, in the same Operation Neuland strike that targeted the Aruba refinery. The bow and stern survived intact, were severed and welded together, towed to the United States, and put back to service as a smaller vessel; only the bombed midsection was abandoned where she ran aground off Palm Beach in 7 to 10 m of water. Twisted plate, exposed boiler housings, and pipe runs sit on a sand and rubble bottom. French grunts, sergeant majors, great barracuda, stoplight parrotfish, and the occasional green moray colonise the steel. Shallow depth and a short boat ride from Palm Beach hotels make it one of the most accessible WWII wreck dives in the Caribbean.
getThere: Queen Beatrix International (AUA) sits on the south side of Aruba with direct flights from major US East Coast hubs, Toronto, Amsterdam, and several Latin American cities. Most Palm Beach operators run the boat trip to the wreck in 15 to 20 minutes, and a handful offer it as a long shore swim from the Palm Beach hotel strip.
notes: Originally a Lake Maracaibo lake tanker built in Monfalcone in 1938; the salvaged bow and stern were welded together and returned to service after the war. The midsection lies inside the buoyed Palm Beach swim and watersports zone, so listen for jet ski and boat traffic before ascending and surface near a marker buoy.

name: Invisible Bank
description: Submerged seamount about 100 km southeast of Havelock Island, where a flat rock crests within 8 m of the surface before its slopes drop past 30 m onto a sand and rubble base. Far from any sheltering land, the site is wholly exposed and the current can rip, but the trade off is pelagic density few Indian Ocean sites match. Dense schools of dogtooth tuna, bluefin and giant trevally, great barracuda, and unicornfish swirl over the rocks while grey reef sharks, whitetips, and resting nurse sharks patrol below. Bumphead parrotfish and napoleon wrasse work the structure, and scalloped hammerheads are sighted on lucky days in late winter. Visibility regularly hits 30 to 40 m. This is an advanced liveaboard dive that runs only when surface conditions cooperate.
getThere: Fly into Veer Savarkar International Airport (IXZ) in Port Blair via Chennai (MAA), Kolkata (CCU), Delhi (DEL), or Bengaluru (BLR). Invisible Bank lies about 100 km southeast of Havelock Island in open ocean and is dived only by liveaboard. Most Andaman liveaboards depart from Port Blair on 7 to 10 day charters and reach the bank as a weather dependent day from anchorage near Inglis or Henry Lawrence Island. Day boat operators based at Havelock can occasionally schedule a long single dive run when the southwest swell is absent.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Current can be unrelenting on the windward face; a reef hook keeps you parked where the pelagic action passes instead of fighting the flow.

siteSpecificGear.name: Long delayed surface marker buoy (closed circuit, 1.8 m or longer)
siteSpecificGear.reason: Pickups happen far offshore in chop. A tall closed circuit SMB with a finger spool is the difference between being seen and not.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nitrox cert with EAN32 fills
siteSpecificGear.reason: Bottom time at 25 to 30 m on air is too short to enjoy the pelagic carousel; EAN32 stretches no deco limits noticeably.

notes: Advanced Open Water with deep training and a verified logbook is the working minimum, and most liveaboard operators want 50 plus logged dives. The site is fully exposed and routinely cancelled when the southwest monsoon swell builds, which can be any time between mid May and mid September. Hammerhead sightings are rare and dependent on bringing thermoclines; treat them as a bonus rather than the headline. Foreign nationals no longer need a Restricted Area Permit for Havelock since 2022 but must carry a passport on board.

name: Pixie Pinnacle
description: A single soft coral spire rising from a sandy 30 m bottom to within 5 m of the surface, no more than 15 m wide at the base. The whole column is wrapped in soft corals and gorgonian fans, with a permanent pink cloud of anthias hovering against the blue. Lionfish patrol caves and overhangs, leaf scorpionfish wedge into coral nubs, magnificent nudibranchs crawl the rubble at the base, and schools of bigeye trevally sweep past the pinnacle. Whitetip reef sharks cruise the sand. Currents are usually mild but can rip on tide changes; the bommie is small enough to circle in one dive. Pixie sits on Ribbon Reef 10 a short distance from Cod Hole and is reached only by liveaboard from Cairns or Port Douglas.
getThere: Cairns (CNS) is the gateway airport, with Port Douglas as the secondary departure point. Pixie Pinnacle is liveaboard only and sits about 140 km north of Cairns on Ribbon Reef 10. Liveaboards run 3, 4, or 7 night Ribbon Reefs itineraries and typically pair Pixie with Cod Hole, Lighthouse Bommie, and Steve's Bommie on Ribbon Reef 3. The nearest land is Lizard Island, about 30 km west, which has its own airstrip served from Cairns.
notes: Marine park fee applies inside the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park. The pinnacle is exposed and tides can drive moderate flow; a reef hook is rarely needed but a surface marker buoy is standard. Dwarf minke whales frequent the wider Ribbon Reefs area in June and July and are sometimes encountered between dives. Combine with the Cod Hole feed dive on the same itinerary.

name: French Reef
description: Six miles southeast of Key Largo inside the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary, French Reef is the cave network of the upper Keys. Three large caverns named Christmas Tree Cave, Sand Bottom Cave, and Hourglass Cave cut through the spur and groove formations between 5 and 12 metres, packed with shimmering schools of glassy sweepers and the occasional resident green moray. The shallow reef carries elkhorn and brain coral, Christmas tree worms erupt from the coral heads, and southern stingrays, nurse sharks, and eagle rays patrol the sand channels. French Deep on the southeast corner drops past 25 metres into deeper water for a wall style finish.
getThere: Fly into Miami International (MIA) and drive about an hour south on US 1 to Key Largo. Fort Lauderdale (FLL) is a backup hub. Most operators depart Key Largo marinas; the reef is a 25 to 30 minute boat ride offshore inside John Pennekamp and the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary.
notes: Sanctuary Preservation Area. Anchoring, spearfishing, lobstering, and coral contact are prohibited; tie to one of the 17 yellow mooring buoys. Reef safe sunscreen required. Several caverns are true swim throughs with sand floors but they can silt out quickly, so a light and good buoyancy help.

name: Million Hope Wreck
description: The largest wreck in the Red Sea. The 175 m Japanese bulk carrier MV Million Hope, built in Hiroshima in 1972 and carrying potash and phosphates from Aqaba, lost power and slammed into the fringing reef at Nabq Bay on 20 June 1996 while crossing the Strait of Tiran. All 25 Filipino crew were rescued. The hull broke its back, rolled to starboard against the reef wall, and now runs from a shattered bow above the surface to a stern propeller at 28 m. Divers usually drop in at the stern crane, encrusted with soft coral and curtained by glassfish, then drift forward over five cavernous holds. Lionfish, scorpionfish, crocodilefish, fusiliers, batfish, and snappers work the rails; nudibranchs and bluespotted stingrays show up along the silted decks.
getThere: Fly to Sharm el-Sheikh International (SSH); the wreck sits roughly 11 km north of the airport off Nabq Bay on the east coast of southern Sinai.
