Most operators run it as a half day boat trip from the Sharm el-Sheikh marinas (Travco, Naama Bay, Sharks Bay) — about a 40 minute crossing — and it is often paired with Jackson Reef or another Tiran Strait site. Shore access via the Nabq Protected Area is also possible by 4WD with a guide, but boat is standard.
notes: Advanced Open Water with wreck experience recommended; the holds are wide and well lit at deck level but full penetration past the bulkheads requires a wreck certification, a primary light, and a backup. Surge near the shallow bow can be sharp in winter winds. Bow plating is sheared and jagged — keep clear of sharp edges and trailing cable. The wreck sits inside the Nabq Protected Area; do not touch coral growth on the crane or rails. Lionfish and scorpionfish are abundant. Nearest hyperbaric chamber is in Sharm el-Sheikh.

name: Hannibal Bank
description: Hannibal Bank rises from more than 400 meters of Pacific abyss to roughly 36 meters below the surface, an isolated triangular guyot sitting about 40 nautical miles west of Coiba Island. Strong oceanic currents sweep the flat top and steep slope, drawing scalloped hammerhead schools through the dry season, whale sharks around January, and big black tips, silky sharks, yellowfin tuna, marlin, and sailfish year round. Divers drop onto rugose seamount terrain in the blue, hook off when current allows, and watch the deeper slope for the occasional tiger shark passing through. Liveaboard or long day boat from Santa Catalina or Boca Chica; advanced certification, deep training, and surface marker buoys required.
getThere: Panama City (PTY) is the international gateway. From there, fly to David (DAV) or drive ~6 hours to Santa Catalina, the main shore base for Coiba. Hannibal Bank lies roughly 40 nautical miles west of Coiba Island in the Gulf of Chiriquí; access is via liveaboard or long day boats out of Santa Catalina or Boca Chica (3 to 4 hours each way), weather permitting.
notes: Open-ocean seamount with strong, unpredictable currents; trips run only in flat seas and many operators require minimum logged dives plus AOW and Nitrox. Surface marker buoys are mandatory. Hannibal Bank sits inside the Coiba Special Marine Management Zone (ZEMP) adjoining Coiba National Park; park fees and ranger registration apply. Nearest hyperbaric chamber is in Panama City.

name: Jessie Beazley Reef
description: Isolated mushroom of reef rising from deep water in the Sulu Sea, roughly 20 km north of the main Tubbataha atolls. The reef cap sits at 7 to 12 m and is carpeted in Acropora and lettuce corals before plunging into a sheer wall that drops past recreational limits. Corner currents stack schooling jacks, barracuda and unicornfish against the wall, while whitetip and grey reef sharks rest on the sand at the base. Tuna, Napoleon wrasse and eagle rays pass through, and hammerheads occasionally cruise the blue in deeper water. Added to the Tubbataha Reefs Natural Park in 2006, it is dived only on calm days during the March through June liveaboard window.

conditionsByMonth.suitRecommendation: Park closed during typhoon and swell season

getThere: Puerto Princesa (PPS) on Palawan has direct flights from Manila and Cebu. Tubbataha liveaboards run a 10 to 12 hour overnight crossing from PPS to the park during the open season from mid March to mid June. Jessie Beazley is dived as a separate stop on most itineraries when conditions allow.
notes: Park is open only from mid March through mid June. A surface marker buoy and reel are mandatory; drift exits over deep water are standard. The reef sits outside the strict core protection zone but inside the expanded park boundary added by Proclamation 1126 in 2006.

name: Quarter Mile Reef
description: The shallowest of the Sodwana reefs sits just off Jesser Point, topping out around 8 m and bottoming near 12 to 13 m on a base of sponges, soft corals, and low ledges. From December through March pregnant ragged tooth sharks gestate here in numbers, hanging motionless above the sand while divers approach on the reef. Outside raggie season the reef carries dense schools of kingfish and rubberlip, ribbontail and honeycomb rays, guitarfish, potato bass in the cracks, and the occasional manta or transiting whale shark. Surface swell can shut the site down on bigger days, but on settled summer mornings vis runs 15 to 25 m.
getThere: Fly into King Shaka International (Durban). Drive about 4 to 5 hours north on the N2 to Hluhluwe, then east through Mbazwana to Sodwana Bay inside iSimangaliso Wetland Park. Boats launch from the beach at Jesser Point and the reef is a 3 minute ride out, just past the breaking surf.
notes: Sodwana boat launches are surf entries — sit on the pontoon, feet hooked under straps. The site closes intermittently during peak raggie season when pregnant females are settled, to limit disturbance. A surface marker buoy is recommended on every dive. iSimangaliso park entry fee applies daily.

name: Sardine Reef
description: An oval seamount rising from 30 m to within 5 m of the surface in the Dampier Strait, where strong tidal currents pull open ocean water across the reef and pile up so much fish biomass that pioneer Max Ammer named the site after the feeling of being packed inside a sardine can. Expect swirling walls of fusiliers, jacks, and snappers, bumphead parrotfish cruising the shallows, and reef sharks patrolling the edges. Soft coral and huge gorgonian fans coat the slopes. Current dictates everything; ride it as a drift.
getThere: Fly to Sorong (SOQ) via Jakarta (CGK) or Bali (DPS) on Garuda, Batik, or Sriwijaya. Transfer to Waisai by ferry (about two hours) or board a Dampier Strait liveaboard or homestay speedboat. Sardine Reef sits roughly fifteen minutes by boat off the eastern tip of Kri Island.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Current accelerates over the seamount; hooking in is the only way to stay put and watch the schools.

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB + reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Drift exits over open water in the strait. Inflate and ascend on the line so boats can find you.

notes: Down currents can pull divers off the wall on full moon tides. Brief carefully, descend fast to escape surface chop, and stay close to the reef. The Raja Ampat marine park entry tag is required; buy it in Waisai or Sorong before you dive.

name: Rocky Island
description: Tiny rock pinnacle 70 km offshore in southern Egyptian Red Sea, the country's southernmost island and the most remote stop on the Deep South liveaboard circuit. Sheer walls plunge from 5 m to over 800 m, fed by open ocean currents that draft pelagic traffic onto the reef. Oceanic whitetip sharks patrol the blue from October through December, often shadowed by pilot fish; scalloped hammerhead schools queue at depth in summer. The east wall is the signature dive: strong drift, grey reef sharks at cleaning stations, schooling barracuda, and big tuna. Liveaboard only.
getThere: Fly into Marsa Alam (RMF) or Hurghada (HRG); both have direct seasonal flights from European hubs. Rocky Island is accessible only by liveaboard. Deep South itineraries depart from Port Ghalib (20 min from Marsa Alam airport) and run 7 to 10 nights, with a 10 to 14 hour open ocean crossing to reach the Foul Bay cluster of Rocky, Zabargad, and St John's.

lodging.label: Port Ghalib Marina hotels (pre and post liveaboard)

siteSpecificGear.name: SMB and long reel
siteSpecificGear.reason: Most dives end as the current drifts you off the wall into open water; every diver shoots their own marker for zodiac pickup.

siteSpecificGear.name: Nitrox certification
siteSpecificGear.reason: Pelagic action happens at 25 to 40 m on the corners; EAN32 buys meaningful bottom time across 3 to 4 dives a day.

siteSpecificGear.name: Reef hook
siteSpecificGear.reason: Strong current sweeps the east and south corners where the sharks queue; hook in to watch without burning gas fighting the flow.

notes: Liveaboard only, no day boats. Foul Bay crossings are committing open ocean; check sea state before booking shoulder season trips. The island is uninhabited and landings are not permitted. Strong currents and the lack of shelter mean the dive guide will call sites based on conditions; if Rocky is unsafe the boat will reroute to Zabargad or St John's. Oceanic whitetips can be inquisitive; brief on shark behavior and avoid trailing gear.
